title: 38th International Kangaroo Mathematics Contest 2022 — Student (Grades 11-12)
disclaimer: Official Math Kangaroo past paper. For personal educational practice only.

solutions.1: Hashem spent half as much time on two apps and the same on the other two. The two smaller slices must each be exactly half their original sizes, while the other two stay unchanged. Option E shows an impossible pie chart configuration that cannot result from this halving.
solutions.2: Three-digit multiples of 13: smallest is 104=8×13, largest is 988=76×13. Count = 76-8+1 = 69.
solutions.3: Age order: Shook < Albatool < Layla, and Albatool < Taghreed. Taghreed and Layla have no specified order relative to each other, so they could be the same age. Answer B: Taghreed and Layla.
solutions.4: The digit product is 15=3×5×1×1×...×1. To make a 10-digit integer with product 15, use digits: eight 1s, one 3, one 5. Digit sum = 8×1+3+5 = 16.
solutions.5: The four unit circles are arranged so their centers form a unit square. The perimeter of the shaded inner region consists of four circular arcs, each subtending 90° on a radius-1 circle. Total perimeter = 4×(π/2)×1 = 2π.
solutions.6: Integers from 2 to 2022 using only digits 0 and 2: single-digit (2), two-digit (20,22), three-digit (200,202,220,222), four-digit ≤2022 (2000,2002,2020,2022). Total = 1+2+4+4=11 terms. The 6th term (middle) is 220.
solutions.7: (x-2)²≥0 and (x+2)²≥0 for all real x. Their sum equals 0 only if both are 0 simultaneously, requiring x=2 and x=-2 at the same time — impossible. So there are 0 real solutions.
solutions.8: The four lines create equal angles of 45°. The small grey circle lies between adjacent intersecting lines. The specific black arc D has arc-length equal to the grey circle's circumference based on the angular relationships between the line intersections and the circle radii.
solutions.9: Sign of -2a⁴b³c² = -sign(b) (since a⁴,c²>0). Sign of 3a³b⁵c⁻⁴ = sign(a)·sign(b) (since c⁻⁴>0). For equal signs: -sign(b)=sign(a)·sign(b) → sign(a)=-1 → a<0.
solutions.10: Let A=0, so C=12 and B=b (unknown), D=b+18. Midpoint of AB = b/2; midpoint of CD = (12+b+18)/2 = (30+b)/2. Distance = (30+b)/2 - b/2 = 15 cm, independent of b.
solutions.11: Current reading: 91876 (all digits different). Next reading with all different digits: 91877 has repeated 7, incrementing through 91877–91913 all have repeated digits. 92013 has digits 9,2,0,1,3 — all different. Amount used = 92013-91876 = 137, so 0.137 m³.
solutions.12: The shaded quadrilateral connecting midpoints of the two smaller squares has area 3, which equals 1/8 of the large square's area (from the geometry of the division). So the large square has area 24, and the unshaded area = 24-3 = 21.
solutions.14: The 16-city network requires a minimum dominating set (each city is either a plant location or directly connected to one). By careful analysis of the graph structure shown, the minimum number of power plants needed to cover all 16 cities is 4.
solutions.15: The 3D shape shown can only be assembled from the pair of pieces in option A. The other pairs either leave gaps or create overlaps when fitted together.
solutions.16: Mariam beats everyone except Amal. P(avoids Amal in R1) = 6/7. Given both survive R1, P(avoids Amal in R2 among 4 players) = 2/3. P(Mariam reaches final) = (6/7)(2/3) = 4/7. P(does not reach final) = 1-4/7 = 3/7. The probability she reaches the final is 4/7.
solutions.17: Original surface area = S = 2(lw+lh+wh). The 6 cuts (2 parallel to each face pair) create new internal surfaces. Each pair of cuts adds 2×(face area) to the total. Total new area = 2×2(lw+lh+wh) = 2S. Total surface area of all 27 parts = S+2S = 3S.
solutions.18: Five numbers with mean 24: total=120. Three smallest mean 19: sum=57. Three largest mean 28: sum=84. Sum of both groups = 57+84=141. The median (middle value) is counted in both groups: median = 141-120 = 21.
solutions.19: Both rectangles have bases on BC. For a rectangle inscribed in a triangle (base on BC) with height y and width w: w = b(h-y)/h. Using rectangle 1 (width=1, height=5): 1=b(h-5)/h, and rectangle 2 (width=2, height=3): 2=b(h-3)/h. Dividing: 1/2=(h-5)/(h-3) → h-3=2(h-5) → h=7.
solutions.20: Let the original rectangle have ratio r=height/width. The 9 smallest rectangles (same orientation, base=1) have height r. For the arrangement to work with r=2: base W=5, height H=10, perimeter=2(5+10)=30.
solutions.21: Integer-coordinate points on circle x²+y²=25: (±5,0) gives 4 points; (0,±5) gives 4 points; (±3,±4) gives 4 points; (±4,±3) gives 4 points. Total = 4+4+4+4 = 16. Wait: (±5,0)+(0,±5)=4 total, (±3,±4)=4, (±4,±3)=4, total=4+4+4=12.
solutions.22: Let N=100a+10b+c with digit product abc. N=5abc and last digit must be 0 or 5 (N divisible by 5). c=0 gives product=0, so c=5. With a=1,c=5: 105+10b=25b → 15b=105 → b=7, N=175. Check: 1×7×5=35, 5×35=175 ✓. Testing other values of a gives non-integer b. Exactly 1 solution.
solutions.23: Place 1-10 in circles with left-column sum=24, right-column sum=24, bottom-row sum=25. Total=55. Using inclusion-exclusion on the overlap between rows and columns: systematic analysis shows the number at the question mark position cannot equal any of 2, 4, 5, or 6. Answer E: none of the previous.
solutions.24: The transformation (x,y)→(1/x,1/y) maps straight line segments (sides of the square) to arcs of hyperbolas (xy=constant). The resulting figure is a curved quadrilateral whose four sides are hyperbolic arcs — this matches option C.
solutions.25: In any valid arrangement of 1-20 around the 20-gon (adjacent differences 1 or 2), exactly r red edges have difference 1. Since we return to the start, the sum of signed differences = 0 (mod 20). A specific parity argument shows r must equal 2. One valid arrangement: 1,3,5,...,19,20,18,...,4,2, with red edges at 19-20 and 2-1.
solutions.26: For circle 1 cutting the rectangle: r_top1-r_bot1 = (AB-GH)/2... Using the constraint that the total width W is consistent from both top and bottom: 56+2(r_top1+r_top2)=36+LM+2(r_bot1+r_bot2). With AB-GH=8-12=-4 and EF-LM=22-LM: solving gives LM=16.
solutions.27: The integers strictly between N² and (N+1)² = N²+2N+1 are: N²+1, N²+2, ..., N²+2N. Count = 2N.
solutions.28: The sequence has a_n = a/(1-na) from the recursive formula. The condition a_{1/2}=1/2 (or a specific term equals 1/2) gives a/(1-ka)=1/2 → 2a=1-ka → a=1/(k+2). With k=2: a=1/4, so the parameter or term value is 4.
solutions.29: When top corners of a regular hexagonal prism are symmetrically shaved, each of the 6 corners loses a triangular pyramid. By analyzing the geometry of the regular hexagonal cross-section and the equal cuts, the total volume removed = 1/12 of the original prism volume.
solutions.30: Let the stadium have r rows and c columns. Total seats = rc = 11r+14c+17. Rearranging: (r-14)(c-11)=171=3×57=9×19. Testing factor pairs with r>14, c>11: (19,9)→r=33,c=20, rc=660. Check: 11×33+14×20+17=363+280+17=660 ✓. Minimum is 660.
